coredata.dc:title: Electrochemical sensor for detecting dopamine using graphene quantum dots incorporated with multiwall carbon nanotubes
coredata.dc:creator: Arumugasamy, Shiva Kumar
Govindaraju, Saravanan
Yun, Kyusik
coredata.dc:description: A ratiometric electrochemical biosensor was developed for dopamine detection based on the incorporation of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with acid-functionalized multiwall carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) on a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) surface. The GQDs@MWCNTs/GCE nanocomposite was characterized by XRD, SEM, TEM, AFM, XPS, and electrochemical techniques. The UV absorbance peak of GQDs was located at 235 nm, and the average size of the GQDs was 1–5 nm. The electrochemical responses indicated that the proposed sensor exhibited good electrocatalytic activity toward the oxidation of dopamine and spiked dopamine, over a dynamic linear range of 0.25–250 μM, with low detection limits of 95 nM and 110 nM (S/N = 3), respectively. In addition, the developed sensor interacted selectively with dopamine rather than other molecules was investigated using an amperometric technique. The developed sensor exhibited excellent reproducibility, selectivity, and sensitivity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: GQDs
MWCNTs
Dopamine
Electrochemical sensor
Nanocomposite

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electrochemical behaviour of dopamine in the GQDs-MWCNTs/GCE nanocomposite
Effect of scan rate and pH
Differential pulse voltammetric analysis of dopamine

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korea Government ( 2017R1A2B4004700 ). This work was supported by the Gachon University research fund of 2019 (GCU-2019-0349). This work was supported by the Gachon University research fund of 2019 (GCU-2019-0704). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of the overall reaction scheme for the synthesis of GQDs@MWCNTs Nanocomposite.
(a) UV pattern of GQDs, MWCNTs and GQDs@MWCNTs nanocomposite; (b) XRD pattern of GQDs, MWCNTs and GQDs@MWCNTs nanocomposite.
TEM and AFM images visualize (a), (d) GQDs; (b), (e) MWCNTs; (c) an(d) (f) GQDs incorporated with MWCNTs nanocomposite. The inset of (c) HR-TEM image of GQDs@MWCNTs.

X-ray photoelectron spectra of as-prepared GQDs@MWCNTs nanocomposite; (a), (e) full range XPS spectrum of before and after dopamine interaction and their individual high-resolution XPS spectra of (b), (f) C1s, (c), (g) N1s and (d), (h) O1s.

(a) Cyclic voltammograms of GQDs@MWCNTs nanocomposite modified GCE in 0.1 M PBS dissolved in PBS of pH = 7.0 in the presence of 2.5 μM dopamine at a various scan rate of 10–100 mV/S. The inset plot shows the relationship between the anodic and cathodic peak current (Ipa, Ipc versus scan rate). (b) Cyclic voltammograms of GQDs@MWCNTs nanocomposite modified GCE in 2.5 μM dopamine dissolved in PBS of varying pH from 2 to 10.

(a) Differential Pulse voltammograms at GQDs@MWCNTs nanocomposite modified GCE for dopamine at various concentrations in PBS of pH = 7.0. The inset is Calibration curve for dopamine for different concentrations, (b) Electrochemical impedance spectroscopic study for GQDs@MWCNTs nanocomposite modified GCE in 1 mM K3Fe(CN)6 and K4Fe(CN)6 along with supporting electrolyte of 0.1 M KCl, (c) Differential Pulse voltammograms at GQDs@MWCNTs nanocomposite modified GCE in the presence of spiked dopamine concentration in PBS of pH = 7.0.

Amperometric responses of the GQDs@MWCNTs modified electrode in the presence of 2.5 μM (a) dopamine, (b) glucose, (c) glutamic acid, (d) KCl and (e) ascorbic acid in 0.1 M PBS (pH = 7.0), applied potential 0.2 V (Ag/AgCl).
Comparison of the developed DA sensor with recent literature.
Detection of dopamine in spiked human serum.

head.ce:title: Electrochemical sensor for detecting dopamine using graphene quantum dots incorporated with multiwall carbon nanotubes

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Bionanotechnology, Gachon University, Gyeonggi-do 13120, South Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Bionanotechnology, Gachon University, Gyeonggi-do 13120, South Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A ratiometric electrochemical biosensor was developed for dopamine detection based on the incorporation of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with acid-functionalized multiwall carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) on a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) surface. The GQDs@MWCNTs/GCE nanocomposite was characterized by XRD, SEM, TEM, AFM, XPS, and electrochemical techniques. The UV absorbance peak of GQDs was located at 235 nm, and the average size of the GQDs was 1–5 nm. The electrochemical responses indicated that the proposed sensor exhibited good electrocatalytic activity toward the oxidation of dopamine and spiked dopamine, over a dynamic linear range of 0.25–250 μM, with low detection limits of 95 nM and 110 nM (S/N = 3), respectively. In addition, the developed sensor interacted selectively with dopamine rather than other molecules was investigated using an amperometric technique. The developed sensor exhibited excellent reproducibility, selectivity, and sensitivity.

ce:section.ce:para: Dopamine (3,4-dihydroxyphenethylamine), an organic chemical belonging to catecholamine and phenethylamine families, acts as both hormone and neurotransmitter, playing a key role in the metabolism of brain and body. In the human body, dopamine functions majorly in the cardiovascular, central nervous, renal, and hormonal systems, in addition to monitoring human metabolism [1]. Imbalances in the dopamine levels lead to serious diseases, such as Parkinson’s disease, attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD), Tourette syndrome, schizophrenia, and bipolar disorder [2]. Dopamine acts on the sympathetic nervous system when ingested intravenously, producing effects such as increased heart rate and blood pressure [3]. Therefore, monitoring the concentration of dopamine is vital both in vivo and in vitro. The dopamine levels in a healthy individual lie in the range of 0.01–10 μM [4].

The various techniques available for the selective and sensitive detection of dopamine include fluorimetry [5,6], chemiluminescence [7,8], capillary electrophoresis [9,10], liquid chromatography [11,12], etc. In comparison with the above-mentioned methods, the electrochemical method has attracted significant interest due to its rapid, reliable, simple, and low-cost process; which occurs because of the direct interaction between the analyte and electrode surface During the past decade, novel biosensors had been developed for detecting various biological and environmental samples either by using enzymes, DNA and conductive nanomaterials (carbon-based, metal-based, noble metals, and polymers) which interacts with the target species generating electrochemical responses. The advantages of electrochemical sensors include flash response, high sensitivity, specificity and selectivity, along with the combination of electrocatalytic activities of the target molecules. In recent years, various analytes such as taxol (anticancer agent), N-acetylcysteine (antidote), hydrochlorothiazide/propranolol (β-adrenergic receptor and diuretic compounds), hydrazine and chlorpromazine (antipsychotic drug) were detected using nanomaterials such as DNA, 2-chlorobenzoyl ferrocene, 1-benzyl-4-ferrocenyl-1H-[1,2,3]-triazole and graphene oxide (GO) respectively [13–17]. Electrochemical sensors for methyldopa (antihypertensive drug) had been detected using nanocomposites made of 5-amino-2′-ethyl-biphenyl-2-ol/carbon nanotubes and Cu/TiO2 [18,19]. Magnetic nanoparticles on combination with multiwall carbon nanotubes was utilized for electrochemical detection of levodopa, in which the usage of MWCNTs enhances the catalytic activity for specific detection of the analyte [20]. Recently, Ganjali et al had reported the detection of dopamine using metal nanocomposite (ZnO/Al2O3), in which Al2O3 have excellent conductivity for oxidizing dopamine into dopamine quinone [21]. Till now research is mainly focused on detecting dopamine and various analyte molecules with lower detection limit and excellent catalytic activities.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), a class of “zero-dimensional” carbon nanomaterials, have gained significant research interest due to their meritorious characteristics such as low toxicity, excellent biocompatibility, simple synthesis, economical advantage, and reliable conductivity [22]. For bio-sensing electrodes, GQDs are an excellent choice due to their oxygen-rich functional groups, remarkable quantum confinement, edge effects, conductivity, stability, biocompatibility, and large surface area [23]. GQDs provide abundant carboxyl groups at the surface, which could significantly enhance the redox response of dopamine. Multiwall carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) have received considerable attention due to their high aspect ratio, excellent chemical and environmental stability, which make them ideal for electrochemical sensor applications. Pristine MWCNTs have low selectivity and sensitivity due to the weak interaction and minimum charge transfer between the MWCNTs and analyte molecules [24]. The improvement of the interfacial interaction is achieved by the surface-functionalization of the nanotubes. The polar groups on the nanotube surface increase adsorption affinity of the electron-donor or acceptor molecules and facilitate good response [25]. Herein, we report that the GQDs@MWCNTs (graphene quantum dots incorporated Multiwall carbon nanotubes) nanocomposite can be employed as electrochemical dopamine sensors. The GQDs increase the electrochemically active surface area of the electrode and the MWCNTs increase the conductivity of the electrode surface. Thus, a novel GQDs@MWCNTs biosensor was prepared for the rapid, simple, and sensitive detection of dopamine. Under optimal conditions, good linearity was achieved from the differential pulse voltammetric peak current, and the dopamine concentration was determined in the range of 250 nM − 250 μM, in a pH 7.0 phosphate buffer saline solution. Meanwhile, the GQDs@MWCNTs nanocomposites were employed for the real sample detection of dopamine.

Glucose, sodium hydroxide, L-dopamine hydrochloride, hydrochloric acid, potassium chloride, ascorbic acid, glutamic acid, ethylenediamine, and phosphate buffer saline (pH = 7.0) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, USA. The MWCNTs were purchased from EMpower solutions, South Korea. Human serum samples were obtained from LEE Biosolutions, USA. A TEM sample grid, with the following specifications: ultrathin carbon type-A, 400 meshes, copper, and a ~42-μm grid-hole, was purchased from Ted Pella, Inc., USA. All the chemicals were used without any further purification. Milli-Q water with a resistance greater than 18 MΩ was used in the experiments.

Ten grams of glucose was mixed with 150 mL of distilled water and sonicated for 15 mins. Subsequently, 4 mL of ethylenediamine and 2 mL of concentrated HCl were added into the flask. The flask was connected to a condenser, and the temperature was maintained at −10 °C. A pump containing isopropyl alcohol was attached to the condenser. The mixture was stirred at 120 °C using a heating mantle, and the reaction time was 2 h. After the reaction, the final mixture was dialyzed using a dialysis membrane (M.W.) 2000 for 2 days. During the dialysis, at 12-h intervals, the membrane solution was concentrated by a rotary evaporator (EYELA SB-1200, USA) and the water was changed. After 2 days, the solution was collected and stored for further analysis.

In this process, 0.5 g of the MWCNTs (with an average diameter of 15 nm and purity >95%) were purchased from the EMPower company, Korea and were functionalized using 0.1 N hydrochloric acids. Firstly, they were sonicated (JAC Ultrasonic 2010, Kodo Technical Research Co., Ltd, Korea) with 20 mL of HCl for 30 mins. Afterwards, the functionalized sample was stirred (WiseStir MSH-20D hotplate stirrer, Miliot Science, Finland) overnight at room temperature. The surface-functionalized MWCNTs were collected, and the mixture was filtered. The resulting precipitate was washed with absolute ethanol and water. Subsequently, the final product was diluted with deionized water, which had been functionalized to contain the –COOH group, and was used for further study.

For the preparation of the GQDs@MWCNTs nanocomposite, 10 mg of the acid-functionalized MWCNTs were dispersed in 20 mL of deionized water, and the resulting mixture was ultrasonicated (Sonics VCX130, Sonics & Materials Inc., Connecticut) for 1 h. Afterwards, 10 mg of the GQDs were added to the dispersed MWCNT solution, and the resulting suspension was probe sonicated for 2 h. Eventually, the homogeneous suspension of the GQDs-incorporated MWCNTs (GQDs@MWCNTs) nanocomposite was obtained (as shown in the schematic image, Fig. 1 ).

The GQDs and as-synthesized GQDs@MWCNTs were diluted in DI water and were subjected to UV–Vis spectroscopy using a Varian Cary 100 UV–Vis spectrophotometer. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained using a Rigaku Rint 2200 series X-ray automatic diffractometer (Cu Kα radiation at a wavelength of 1.5406 Å) from Texas, USA. FESEM images were documented using JEOL S-4700 multi-beam system having in-lens detector system aimed to a resolution of 1.6 nm with a low accelerating voltage of 1 kV and also utilizes electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD) for energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS). HRTEM images were recorded using Tecnai™ G2 F30 series the ultimate nano-analysis system, which was equipped with a 300 kV Schottky FEG with a high maximum beam current, patented S-TWIN objective lend, HR-STEM resolution of 0.17 nm, point resolution of 0.20 nm, and line resolution of 0.102 nm, was purchased from Hillsboro, USA. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) was conducted using a JPK NanoWizard II bio atomic force microscope with the sample placed on the slide and measured according to the protocol, at 20 kV, and in the contact mode. XPS spectra were recorded using a Thermo Scientific K-AlphaTM+ X-ray photoelectron spectrometer system equipped with a 100–4000 eV motion range, 180° double-focusing hemispherical analyzer– 128-channel detector, and an Al-Kα micro-focused X-ray source, obtained from Thermo Scientific, USA.

The electrochemical properties of all the samples were studied by cyclic voltammetry (CV), differential pulse voltammetry (DPV), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), and amperometry using a VERSASTAT3 system with a three-electrode configuration containing a GCE as the working electrode, and the Ag/AgCl electrode and platinum wire as the reference and counter electrodes, respectively. Prior to drop-casting, the GCE was polished with alumina powders, subsequently rinsed thoroughly, and finally dried. A 10 μL suspension of the GQDs, MWCNTs, and GQDs@MWCNTs were drop-casted separately on the pre-treated bare GCE. All the electrochemical experiments were conducted in a phosphate buffer saline (PBS) solution (pH = 7.0) in the potential range of −0.2 to + 0.8 V. A reproducible voltammogram was obtained under steady-state conditions.

The absorption spectrum of the GQDs incorporated with MWCNTs was employed for the preliminary confirmation. Quantum dots (QDs) are semiconductor nanocrystals or nanoparticles, usually confined by the motion of the conduction band electrons and valence band holes. The absorbance peak for GQDs was located at 235 nm, corresponding to the π–π* electronic transitions of the aromatic C-C bonds of the graphene molecule [26]. GQDs mostly consist of sp2-hybridized carbon, and they are amorphous in nature. The acid-functionalized MWCNTs show a characteristic UV spectrum peak at 242 nm, which is attributed to the free-electron plasmon resonance of the π electronic transition present in the nanotubes. The incorporation of GQDs with MWCNTs forms a nanocomposite via π-π interactions; subsequently, the peak shift absorbed in 247 nm, showing the interaction between the sp2 hybridized GQDs region and the MWCNTs surface (Fig. 2 a) [27].

X-ray diffraction analysis was used to determine the crystalline nature and phase purity of the synthesized GQDs@MWCNTs nanocomposite. The obtained XRD peak patterns are compared with those of the GQDs and MWCNTs. The broad reflections of the peaks at 2θ = 16.03° correspond to the carbon peaks (glucose) used as a source in the synthesis of GQDs. The reflection could be related to the (0 0 2) plane that corresponds to the high distribution of interlayer carbon packing. Interestingly, the carbon material peaks are also present as a reflection at low angle scattering, originating from either the intramolecular repeat distance or the inter-planar distance [28]. The synthesized GQDs show a sharp peak at 2θ = 22.56°, which is attributed to the thinness and disordered stacking of some GQDs. The MWCNTs exhibit two peaks one at 2θ = 22.56° and the other at 2θ = 25.6° (0 0 2), 43.03° (1 0 0) plane. The XRD pattern of the nanocomposite is clearly depicted, and the major diffraction peaks at 22.6° and 26.19°, corresponding to the (0 0 2) and (1 0 0) planes of the GQDs incorporated with the MWCNTs, were observed [29]. The observed XRD pattern shows some broad peaks, suggesting that the materials have a disordered and amorphous structure (Fig. 2b).

HRTEM and AFM analyses were performed to verify the structural characteristics of the GQDs (Fig. 3 a) before (Fig. 3b) and after the incorporation of the MWCNTs in aqueous solution (Fig. 3c). The HRTEM images revealed that the obtained GQDs had spherical structures, with an average size of about 1–5 nm [30]. The AFM images confirm that the average size of the GQDs is 1–5 nm; thus, it could be concluded that most of the GQDs are single or double-layered (Fig. 3d–f). The height profiles of the obtained GQDs are in the range of 4–6 nm and are evenly distributed [31]. Furthermore, we examined the morphologies of the MWCNTs and the MWCNT-incorporated GQDs by HRTEM and AFM. The network of MWCNTs was distributed homogeneously, and it could be clearly visualized. Upon incorporation of GQDs with MWCNTs, a strong bond was formed due to the π-π stacking and electrostatic interactions, which were clearly visualized. FESEM, EDS and elemental mapping analyses of acid-functionalized MWCNTS and nanocomposite (GQDs@MWCNTs) had also been performed and their images are provided in the supplementary information (Figs. S2 and S3) respectively. Since both of our materials are highly carbonaceous, we were able to observe carbon and oxygen present in major quantity in our sample.

The surface chemical composition of the GQDs@MWCNTs modified electrode was analyzed by XPS before and after the grafting of dopamine. The full range of the GQDs@MWCNTs XPS survey spectra before and after the grafting of dopamine is depicted in Fig. 4 (a) and (e), which illustrate the binding energy of all elements, including C, O, and N. In the full range of the spectrum before the grafting of dopamine, the binding energies at 286 eV, 401 eV, and 533 eV correspond to C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s, respectively. In Fig. 4b), the deconvolution of the C 1s spectra demonstrates the following binding energy values: 284 eV CC/sp2 and CC/sp3, 285 eV CO, 287 eV CO, 288.5 eV OCO, and 291 eV for the π–π interactions [32]. The N 1s region (Fig. 4c) was fitted to two peaks at 401.2 eV for ternary amine groups and 403.5 eV for amide groups. After the grafting of dopamine, peak shifts were observed in the full range of the spectrum, indicating the interaction between dopamine and the GQDs@MWCNTs nanocomposite. A shift in the peak was observed at 288.5 eV with an atomic percentage of 4.79%, which was identified as CO, corresponding to the oxidation of the phenolic group (Fig. 4g) in the grafted dopamine. Additionally, 284.5 eV CC can be identified as a phenolic group with an atomic percentage of 15.32% [33]. In the spectra, the O 1s peak (Fig. 4d and h)) was observed in both cases at 531.2 eV and 532.5 eV, corresponding to the C-O-H group based on the literature reports [34,35]. Interestingly, the atomic percentage of the former was increased compared to that of the latter, indicating that a large amount of dopamine experienced grafting.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical behaviour of dopamine in the GQDs-MWCNTs/GCE nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: The cyclic voltammograms (CV) for the GQDs, MWCNTs, and GQDs@MWCNTs were obtained without (Fig. S4a) and with (Fig. S4b) dopamine in a 0.1 M PBS solution with pH = 7.0. For the bare GCE, no noticeable oxidation and reduction peak appeared. For the GQDs@MWCNTs/GCE nanocomposite, the oxidation and reduction peaks were located at 0.15 V and 0.18 V, respectively. Following the addition of 2.5 μM dopamine to the electrolyte, further characterization of GQDs/GCE, MWCNTs/GCE, and GQDs@MWCNTs/GCE were conducted, and the reduction peak potentials were observed at 0.2 V, 0.18 V, and 0.15 V, respectively [36,37]. A pronounced difference was observed in the electrodes upon modification with the nanocomposites, which indicated a quasi-reversible electrochemical behavior on the GCE. The change in the potential shift was owing to the addition of dopamine, which interacted with the electrode interfacial surface. The incorporation of the GQDs to MWCNTs resulted in further amplification of the cathodic current by a factor 4 times higher than that of the bare electrode due to the relatively high electrochemically active surface area of the GQDs@MWCNTs nanocomposite formed. An excellent electrochemical environment was provided for the nanocomposite (GQDs@MWCNTs), which also acted as a barrier for other interfering molecules during the detection of dopamine. Graphene dots have a carboxyl group with a negative charge, which increases the conductivity of the electrode surface and enables the specific interaction between the GQDs and dopamine.

On further analysis of the GQDs@MWCNTs nanocomposite with varying dopamine concentrations, the cyclic voltammogram response shows that the anodic and cathodic peak currents increased linearly, indicating the quasi-reversible process in the PBS solution of pH = 7.0. The increase in the cathodic peak current is due to the oxidation of dopamine to dopamine quinone. Fig. S5 shows that the GQDs@MWCNTs nanocomposite possesses excellent electrocatalytic activity toward dopamine oxidation. This enhanced electrocatalytic response was mainly attributed to the relatively large electroactive surface area of the nanocomposite modified layer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of scan rate and pH
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the electrode reaction dynamics, the scan rate effect was demonstrated. Fig. 5 (a) shows the cyclic voltammogram of the GQDs@MWCNTs nanocomposite-modified electrode in the 0.1 M PBS solution (pH = 7.0) at different scan rates from 10 to 100 mV/S. The inset of Fig. 5(a) shows the linear response between the anodic and cathodic peak current vs. scan rate, indicating that the electro-redox reaction of dopamine on GQDs@MWCNTs/GCE was a diffusion-controlled process. The linear regression equations are Ipa = 4.7903γ1/2 – 9.2065 (R2 = 0.99003) and Ipc = −4.2846γ1/2 + 13.6769 (R2 = 0.9899), respectively.

Fig. 5(b) shows the effect of pH on the oxidation of 2.5 μM dopamine in the 0.1 M PBS solution at the GQDs@MWCNTs-modified electrode, determined by CV in the pH range of 2–10. The negative shift of the dopamine redox peaks with an increase in pH show linearity for Ep′= (Epa + Epc)/2) and pH (2–10). The linear regression equation is given as Ep′ = 0.6547–0.0656 pH (R2 = 0.9925), which indicates that the redox reaction occurred with the transfer of two electrons and two protons, suggesting that it obeys the Nernst equation [38]. From SI Fig.. S6, the maximum oxidation current of dopamine was notable at pH 7.0, and this pH was chosen for the rest of the electrochemical dopamine sensing in this study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Differential pulse voltammetric analysis of dopamine
ce:section.ce:para:
To explore the sensitivity and the limit of detection (LOD), DPV analysis of dopamine using the GQDs@MWCNTs/GCE nanocomposite under optimized conditions was carried out. Fig. 6 (a) shows the well-defined and stable anodic oxidation peak curves of the GQDs@MWCNTs-modified electrode in the 0.1 M PBS solution (pH = 7.0) for different concentrations of dopamine (250 nM–250 μM). Here, the DPV curve was recorded between the potential of 0 and 0.4 V at an amplitude of 0.025 V, a step potential of 0.05 V, and a scan rate of 50 mV/S. The DPV curve shows a well-defined and stable anodic peak current for dopamine [39]. The obtained results demonstrate that the GQDs@MWCNTs-modified electrode exhibits good electrocatalytic activity toward dopamine sensing. In addition, the DPV current response increased linearly with the dopamine concentration in the range of 250 nM–250 μM (Fig. 6a, inset) with a correlation coefficient of 0.9968, and the modified electrode exhibits excellent sensitivity with the dopamine concentration, with a LOD of 95 nM. The LOD was calculated using the following formula:
L O D =
3 S
q
where S is the standard deviation, and q is the slope of the calibration curve. The comparison of the dopamine determination on the proposed sensor with that on the previously reported sensors is provided in Table 1 . The GQDs@MWCNTs/GCE nanocomposite exhibits good sensitivity and improved performance for dopamine determination.

Fig. 6 b shows the EIS results in the form of Nyquist plots. The impedance spectra are characterized by a semicircle portion at higher frequencies, which signifies the kinetics of the redox system. Conversely, a linear portion at lower frequencies corresponds to the diffusion process at the interface described by the Warburg resistance. A large semicircle is observed for the bare GCE, suggesting a relatively high electron transfer resistance offered to the ferricyanide redox probe. When the GQDs are drop-casted on the electrode surface, the barrier of the interfacial electron transfer is reduced, resulting in a small semicircle. On further investigation with MWCNT incorporation, the electron transfer resistance is reduced, and a comparatively high electronic conduction occurs through the electrode/electrolyte. Following the composite (GQDs@MWCNTs) modification, the conductivity is further increased. The conductivity of the modification follows the order of GQDs@MWCNTs > MWCNTs > GQDs at the surface of GCE [38]. Our composite provides excellent active sites for the redox probes. The parameters that are obtained after fitting the impedance data to the equivalent circuit model are provided in the Table. 2 , where Rs = solution resistance, Rct = charge-transfer resistance, Cdl = double-layer capacitance, and W = Warburg resistance.

To screen the practical applicability, the target analytes in the real samples (human serum) were assayed. The serum sample was spiked with 0.5–10 μM dopamine, and the DPV analysis was performed using standard addition methods. The recoveries of dopamine in the human serum were calculated from Fig. 6 c and tabulated in Table. 3 . Thus, the proposed electrochemical system shows promise for potential dopamine detection application in biological samples via a quick and simple process.

To determine the specificity of the present study, the selective detection of dopamine relative to several species such as glucose, KCl, ascorbic acid, and glutamic acid was evaluated. As shown in Fig. 7 , the electrochemical sensing of dopamine was only initiated upon addition of dopamine, which was measured using the amperometry technique at a potential of +0.2 V in PBS (pH = 7.0). Particularly, the similarity of the electrochemical properties of ascorbic acid to those of dopamine did not affect the current density of the GQDs@MWCNTs/GCE nanocomposite. These results clearly demonstrate the high electrochemical detection specificity for dopamine using the GQDs@MWCNTs/GCE nanocomposite.

The reproducibility was investigated from the current responses using 2.5 μM dopamine with 3 electrodes, prepared under identical conditions, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) was 2.48%, indicating the excellent reproducibility of the sensor.

In this study, a ratiometric electrochemical sensor based on a GQDs@MWCNTs nanocomposite-modified electrode was developed for dopamine detection. The introduction of MWCNTs not only improved the electrochemical signal of dopamine but also increased the GQDs catalytic activity. The dual signal enhancement facilitated the construction of a ratiometric electrochemical sensor for the sensitive and accurate detection of dopamine. The high sensitivity toward dopamine detection and the signal enhancement of the GQDs@MWCNTs composite were reasonably explained. The results show that the high sensitivity of GQDs@MWCNTs nanocomposite toward dopamine detection predominately originates from the surface coverage of dopamine, along with the signal enhancement and electrocatalytic activity. This sensor, based on GQDs@MWCNTs/GCE, displays a wide linear range from 250 nM to 250 μM with a LOD of 95 nM. In addition, the developed electrochemical sensor displays high selectivity, good reproducibility, and good stability; moreover, its dopamine detection applicability in human serum samples was also studied.

Shiva Kumar Arumugasamy: Conceptualization, Methodology, Resources, Validation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Saravanan Govindaraju: Conceptualization, Methodology, Resources, Validation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing, Project administration. Kyusik Yun: Conceptualization, Writing - review & editing, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korea Government (2017R1A2B4004700). This work was supported by the Gachon University research fund of 2019 (GCU-2019-0349). This work was supported by the Gachon University research fund of 2019 (GCU-2019-0704).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2020.145294.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Biogenic amine transmitters: acetylcholine, norepinephrine, and dopamine
Vet. Psychopharmacol.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T.F. Murray, Biogenic Amine Transmitters: Acetylcholine, Norepinephrine, and Dopamine, Vet. Psychopharmacol. (2019) 29-42.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A facile one-step electrochemical fabrication of reduced graphene oxide–multiwall carbon nanotubes–phosphotungstic acid composite for dopamine sensing
J. Electroanal. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.Y. Ling, Q.A. Huang, M.S. Zhu, D.X. Feng, X.Z. Li, Y. Wei, A facile one-step electrochemical fabrication of reduced graphene oxide–multiwall carbon nanotubes–phosphotungstic acid composite for dopamine sensing, J. Electroanal. Chem. 693 (2013) 9-15.
